item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, I have no words. I never even thought an article of this quality would be even achievable.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The friendship and adventure of two men in SCP format. Its distinctive delight and warmth are characteristic of SCP-2188, which is difficult to find in other SCPs.

This is my favorite SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this some years ago, before I bit the bullet and made an account in order to upvote. This was one of about five or six that I remember quite readily and kept meaning to find again to +1.

So +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An eerily beautiful tale in the shape of an SCP. Very well done, this was really a breath of fresh air.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whew…that was beautiful, and I'm glad to have read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I do genuinely agree with the comments noting that this feels a lot like a tale, the fact that I did not notice this until reading the comments says something. I feel like this piece stretches the boundaries of what really gets to go in an SCP, and it gets away with it in beautiful style.

This is the quality of work I wish to produce one day for this site. This shit is why I joined SCP.

Well played sir. Well played indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a strong contender for best-written work on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It may be a tale in a skip's skin, but that doesn't matter, because it gave me feels and a soft cry. Take my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i must admit. I was expecting a more comical work.

Instead I got a heartfelt tale of a larger then life artist and the community he had unknowingly effected.

Tears were shed by the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My heart is full. Thank you, Kal — thank you. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The weird part of reading this for me was the very strong feeling that I was reading a new work by Daniel Pinkwater, which is high praise indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad I finally stumbled upon this. +1 forever and I have no words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel this article itself is another work of art affected by SCP.
That said, I appreciate and admire the amount of effort you put into this, but forgive me, this is just tooo long for me to read from top to bottom. I had to skim through it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Touching, unique and beautifully written, though the tone is reeeeeaaaally off here. An easy fix, however, would be to mention that the narration is taken from the agent's personal diary, and not from his field reports.

In spite of this, +1

A few mistakes I found in the text
- Hide

I remarked to him at our last meeting that the poets of La Figurita weren't very good,

I had remarked

He explained that all I need do was accompany him for a few hours.

all I needed to do

He is not wrong, but I was dismayed at his reading of me.

was not wrong

He with the face that belonged in a prison yard, but I had to struggle to hear him as he read his piece.

I suggest "He had a face that belonged in a prison yard, yet I had to struggle to hear him as he read his piece."

Instead of answering, he instead directed me to a large clearing behind the cathedral.

two instances of "instead"

It may amaze the researchers reading these dispatches, but I have not had need to be duplicitous with Pablo in the many years I have been his keeper.

"I had not had need", "in the many years I had been his keeper."

Quickly, to change the subject. Why self-portraits?

I suggest: Quickly, to change the subject: "Why self-portraits?"

Also, these are portraits, self-portraits have to be painted by the subject of the portrait, which is not the case here (additionally, while the paintings are portraits, they are not portraits of Pablo, even if they were intended to be).

"Not yet!" he cried, averting his gaze, "it must all happen at once, a grand unveiling of a collected truth!" he pronounced to the room, laughing at the grandiose statement, but I could tell also deadly serious.

I suggest adding commas: "laughing at the grandiose statement, but, I could tell, also deadly serious."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
albeit in a manner consistent with anthropological norms documented in mainstream science.

Anthropology, at least in the West, isn't normative, and wouldn't have been (as of 1998) for several decades.

As for the larger piece, it failed to really affect me in any way. I'm not a big fan of "geographically disparate things that are anomalously connected for no apparent reason" as a thing (see also: SCP-2972). Disregard. I don't mind the format/tone so much, but I just wasn't particularly concerned with Mr. Izquierdo or his actions. Some of the scenes were quite well-written, but it fell flat for me. Towards the end, I was mostly just ready for it to be over. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am Uruguayan, and I can say I enjoyed this a lot. The story really captured the late 20th century culture of Montevideo (for those who don't know, during the period the skip describes we were under a pretty ugly dictatorship, which caused a lot of anarchic artists to pop up), and the character of Izquierdo feels very realistic (we are that insane, yes).

A little niptick though: You don't put more than one straw in a mate. Everybody drink from a single one. (Yeah, it isn't very hygienic, but silver straws aren't cheap)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is beautiful in a very geographically appropriate magical realism way, though my less sentimental side questions whether this is even statistically correlative, on the other hand my more literary side is saying that the other voice is missing the point…
I feel like I should have read this before, but I know that I have not. Funny how the heart knows things the brain does not. We are better designed, that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing is lovely. The story told here is worthwhile, memorable and thoroughly enjoyable. And the human subject is made to appear touching and sympathetic without "cheating", cheap cheesy tearjerking or cutting corners. Also SCPs having unusual nontrivial nature is always a big plus in my eyes (i.e. it's an anomalous relationship between a person and a town after all, not just a person or an object or an event). All the tone breaking is done in the optional field reports, which seems like fair game to me, especially considering how meaningfully and skillfully it is used here. So I upvoted with very little hesitation. I'm not sure about in general, but as an atypical SCP I'd say this is pretty exemplary. I'd welcome reading something like this once in a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an exceptional example of a long-form SCP. For those unhappy about tone, I would note that everything through the Description is perfectly in keeping with Foundation norms. The beauty of the format is that Addenda can be in any style, provided there is a reason for them to exist. The field reports make sense in the context of this SCP, but of course their reason to exist is the elegaic beauty of the writing.

I'm not sure yet whether this is my favourite SCP, but it is the article that has caused me to start a list of my favourites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, I, uh, wow. This is absolutely beautiful. Upvoting immediately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only very minor complaints (I blame Reach for these expectations) is that I wish Roberto's comments were in fancy different boxes, and maybe add a small paragraph at the start, to drive the point home that these are not his field reports as much as reminiscences that he was asked to provide in order to try to make out an explanation of the phenomena in Dotson (if it weren't for a few loose lines, I can totally see all of these being taken post-retirement). Oh, and I do agree the discovery of the anomaly is a bit of a stretch.

But other than that, there's no other way for this article to really work. Sure, you have a technically complete (in-universe) article without the collapsibles, and if you paraphrased Esquivel's comments you'd have a perfectly good, interesting article. But having the commentary be as it is, completely natural (to anyone who claims it feels too forced: go hang out with a bunch of bohemians, you'll find at least two people who talk like this) chronicles of important events in Izuierdo's life, makes it beautiful. The juxtaposition is necessary, separating the main article from the commentary would take away from both.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And then some assholes do the literal, exact opposite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude, most of the explicitly named places in SCPs are specifically chosen so that they don't actually exist and nobody can check up on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no such place as Dotson Nebraska, at least according to my research

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To the people complaining about the tone, it's very accurate. Even for an oficial report.
Have a little faith and believe me when I tell you that it grasped the spirit of the lands that I live in.

Also, Borges. You said the characters hated him. But the way you wrote this piece says otherwise to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic. I have very little to say beyond praising the prose, which reads both earnest and genuinely latin-american, and how it absolutely accomplishes what I can surmise is its intended effect. What's more, the atypical, expressive, meaningful prose, coming not just from an SCP agent but one posing as an accountant, no less, could be read both as an extension of the skips effect/actual cause, or the genuine, heartfelt sentiments of an honest friend and companion, the ambivalence/combination of which further taps into the general theme of art as mysterious expression that the skip very effectively alludes to.

It is fantastic, and much, much better than anything I've seen from those pretentious, elitist idiots of AWCY. They wish their keter classes were as effective as your skip is at transmitting the meaningfulness of art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I figured out my qualm with this: it reads less like an SCP and more like the memoirs of a Foundation agent writing in a Lifted Veil world. However, it would be criminal to downvote something as well-written as this, and, given the context of Foundation Fiction in general and the quality of this piece, I am more than willing to suspend my disbelief and push its rating further into the positives. GG, Kalinin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the thing I most appreciate about this entry is that the Foundation is competent enough to balance risk, economics and ethics to the extent that they afforded this minor reality bender with the containment method that was presented.

I feel that a different, more cynical iteration of the Foundation would have just put Pablo and the town into conventional containment. Instead, we have an Agent who was chosen for his particular artistic and social sympathies and got a "Big Fish" esque tale that evokes some of the great features that I've found through my limited exposure to Chicano and Latin American literature.

This is such a pleasant surprise on this site and I really enjoy this evolution of the format without compromising tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the desire to have good writing on this site. That is why, in all of my comments in this thread, I acknowledge that this is an exceptionally well written piece.

I enjoy the inventive, long form character study. That is why I am such a big fan of your work, Kate McTiriss. And I know it's ballsy for some random who hasn't attempted a piece in two years deliver that sort of criticism to a well respected prolific writer, but frankly I believe I owe it to the author when something distracts me enough to prevent me from enjoying their work. That is not to discourage good writing. I am glad that it encourages this sort of discussion, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although I can clearly see how this is anomalous as it is not typical for such a wide scale and of artistic influence, I reckon it doesn't really conform to what the Foundation would perceive to be their brand of anomaly such that it is classified and studied.
This is a beautiful story. It provides a recount of an experience that shows us a fictional existence with well described atmosphere and ideologies, and I can feel myself pulled into the story. It is well written.
I can also, however, see why people have an issue. It is a good tale. It is, however, not very Founation-esque, and not very SCP report like. But I don't mind. I am, however, an abstractionist who very much does not care for format. It is also that reason I do not write on this site. How valuable my opinion is, you may decide, but I say this is a good job done.
Thanks for the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rather than critique the comment, I'll try responding to it. <.<

The SCP Foundation wiki presents a unique challenge for the writer: craft a story in a highly rigid, faux non-fiction format. Those who find the article format overly restrictive can turn to tales to exercise their prose, but otherwise, a tale is a tale and a skip is a skip. You can have tale-like aspects to an article, but when they overtake the article, as is the case here, the question becomes, "Why bother writing an article at all?"

To an author who can't deal with the scientific article format, I say, try harder. Lots of people have written great stories as articles without ever once breaking tone. There have been lengthy pieces of fiction tied to articles, and even in the body thereof. But they need to adhere to the format first and foremost, because that's the challenge here. In this case, the article comes off as an excuse to have these lengthy passages of completely non-professional introspection. I mean, the writing's good, but it's more "literary canon" than "notes of Foundation field agent". When telling a story requires that level of removal from the standard tone, then it's time to move into the realm of tales.

And I mean, I like this skip. I like the effects, it's extremely unique, it's a good idea. But the format is not the kind of thing I want to see on this site, not under an "SCP" heading anyway, and i fear that if we promote this sort of thing, series four will just end up being stories with numbers instead of articles.

tl;dr: I've been around for a couple years and I'm getting antsy because things are changing and I don't like them and I'm also mad because another writing community I'm in is having issues with people who don't want to write under established strictures and you should probably just ignore me. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate to be so "Vague" with my comments but all I can say is this: Great Work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saying this work — this sort of inventive, long-form character study — doesn't belong here doesn't just devalue Kalinin's immense achievement here, but devalues all of our work.

Granted, there is a substantial number of people out there who don't like this sort of SCP, and they're free to feel that way. Most feel hesitant to voice that opinion, though, since it's in the relatively obvious minority so far as discussion posts go (it's the most common concern I hear about in PMs from newbies, who are worried about responses to their forum posts).

I agree that stories are important and tale-like SCPs are great, but I do feel there's room for people to favor the "classic" SCPs, or their own preference for SCPs, without it seeming like the writing they don't like is devalued for the rest of the site members. Like and don't like what you want, y'know.

That said, I do like where this is going, after a quick skim. I personally found the length of this article rather daunting, but I'm not going to vote on it until I have some time to read the entire piece in one sitting. Midterms are murder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's quite possibly just that it's being used as "smash in a bunch of extra info that would otherwise break tone." Your thing is a rock what extracts people's souls? Have the article explain about prolonged contact deadening emotional responses, then link to a footnote: "See also: 'Soul Removal and Links to Higher Brain Functions: On the Effects of SCP-XXXX". It's a cheat.

Now maybe they aren't all used like that, but I've quickly grown to discount them the moment I see them because they have been. I also think they're being overused quite a lot; I could see one, maybe two in a medium-length article, but some have upwards of five, for crying out loud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I started reading, I felt like what I was looking at wasn't anomalous at all. Then you went and convinced me otherwise.

I don't think I like this all that much, but I do find it very good. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's convoluted, it's awesome and HOW THE HELL DID YOU THINK OF THIS!?!
I loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Will I do agree with some of the complaints (the connection between Izquierdo and Dotson is made a bit too easily, Esquivel's writing is a bit florid, et cetera) these pale in comparison to the beauty of the work. Easily one of your best.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You just had to mention Borges, didn't you?

Anyways, this is really excellent. I'd love to see more like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty floored. I didn't show up at the wiki today expecting to find an gradually-unfolding magical-realist narrative about an inconvenient friendship between two men and the uncanny outlet for their feelings under the surface.

When people say that the hallmark of Series 3 SCPs is the elevation of the format beyond the telling of fun, easily-digested flash fiction into something more complex, moving, and impressive, this is the sort of thing they're referring to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Their opinions only represent what they feel, not what the site as a whole feels. It's not cool to call them out for telling the author what they personally didn't like about the article.

KateMcTiriss is responding to those three people specifically. It's perfectly acceptable to respond to someone's comment in a discussion thread. The only thing that makes this post different from all the other replies is that, instead of responding in three separate posts, she's responding in one, and quoting those people to show the specific statements she's responding to. I see nothing wrong with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All three of these people have made comments expressing their own opinions without violating the criticism policy.

This part is absolutely correct, and I welcome their criticism. I may not share the views of these users, but you are right in pointing this part out.

It's not cool to call them out for telling the author what they personally didn't like about the article. They can downvote what they want to so long as it's because of the attributes of the work.

This part, though, I think is missing the discussion. I don't think McTiriss was saying that they were somehow in violation of established criticism guidelines or that they were downvoting on something other than merit.

If you see things such as these as a problem facing the site, then you should create a thread for it. There's no place on a skip discussion thread comment for addressing major creative issues at the expense of other readers. (emphasis mine)

I don't think that the italicized statement is accurate. In fact, I think particular articles can be a helpful place to discuss larger sitewide trends in what the readership is looking for and where a piece fits within that expectation (articles such as SCP-2845 and SCP-2718 come to mind).

I wouldn't object to a separate thread to discuss this sort of thing, but I don't think anyone in the thread is out of order here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And Kate is perfectly allowed to express her disagreement with that.
Because that's how critique works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will second this.

Now, this particular scip didn't necessarily grip me. It's not my proverbial cup to tea. I cannot see myself writing a tale about it, incorporating it into my headcanon, or anything along those lines. It originated in a worldview of the Foundationverse that I brook no favor in, is written in a style I care nothing for, is written by someone I very openly admit my dislike of, but it is phenomenally well written.

I upvoted it accordingly, because we need more well-written things on the site.

I will offer the same rebuttal that I use when my own scips are claimed to be "too tale like".

That's the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All three of these people have made comments expressing their own opinions without violating the criticism policy. Their opinions only represent what they feel, not what the site as a whole feels. It's not cool to call them out for telling the author what they personally didn't like about the article. They can downvote what they want to so long as it's because of the attributes of the work. If you see things such as these as a problem facing the site, then you should create a thread for it. There's no place on a skip discussion thread comment for addressing major creative issues at the expense of other readers.

Despite all of that, I actually don't disagree with the sentiment of the part of your post regarding the article and am definitely upvoting this skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why can we not upvote comments, damnit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would upvote this thrice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, so, let's spoil this at the start: I'm in love with this, enthusiastic +1.

The feedback to this SCP is disheartening. Let's run through some of the comments here:

I like the general idea, but quite honestly, tl;dr - TL333s

This is beautifully written… Too beautifully written. The sheer lack of Foundation tone in Esquivel's notes is befitting a tale (and a very good tale), but not a scip file. - dajvg

Secondly, the agent comments feel way too tale-like - Ihpkmn

This is the sort of thing that makes me wonder — what exactly are we looking for, on this site, if not this? This is magical realist fiction with deep characterization, immense creativity and high quality.

Saying this work — this sort of inventive, long-form character study — doesn't belong here doesn't just devalue Kalinin's immense achievement here, but devalues all of our work. Are we a flash-fiction site for easily digestible straight sci-fi? I think we can achieve something more here. I think there's room on the site for narratives like this. And I hope to see more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Too convoluted for my tastes. It seems like this sort of wild cross referencing would produce millions of false positives a day.

I am glad, however, that everyone else's suspension of disbelief isn't as neurotic as mine and this brilliant writing isn't wasted in them. This one made me go back and read more of Kalinin's work, which has been a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, I'm still not a fan of the inter-Foundation publication footnotes

As one of the original offenders, I'm really starting to wonder what exactly it is you have against the Foundation having internal publications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While an alright concept, the entire presentation irks me in a way that's hard to describe. It feels too… off to work as an SCP, almost, at least the way it's executed here. It started out strong, but then I got to this part:

Mr. Izquierdo came to the attention of Foundation assets based in the Buenos Aires Regional Office in 1965, when he was arrested by Uruguay's Interior Police for attempting to smuggle live specimens of the Paraguaian hairy dwarf porcupine into Argentina.

And any suspension of disbelief exited the building forthwith.

Secondly, the agent comments feel way too tale-like. This feels like it should be separated into two things, the SCP itself, and the supplement or a separate tale that contains the narrative presented in the agent comments. At the very least, they belong in quote blocks.

That, and, I'm sorry, but S-TARC is just the dumbest concept I have ever heard for a task force of any kind. "Transreality Art Research Collective"? Have Task Forces really gotten so specific that we need a task force to analyze art from another universe? What next, a task force that looks at anomalous elbow macaroni? MTF-Psi-8, "The Twizzlers", which contain anomalous liquorice? The point is, it just seems silly.

Overall, I don't terribly like it. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it's a bit of stretch regarding how the foundation discovered this in the first place, but quite frankly the story's good enough for that not to matter to me. Great work.

Edit: I should probably say that a phrase such as "good enough" doesn't exactly convey how much I like this. I honestly think this is as successful as the likes of 1425 and 342 in terms of longer stories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd assume that the Foundation would keep tabs on local criminal activity to detect potential anomalies, and when they noticed the denizens of a town performing some really bizarre ritualistic dance routine involving porcupines on the same day that a guy attempted to smuggle a bunch of porcupines (unless I'm mistaken, this doesn't seem like a normal occurrence), they at least suspected something was up. Afterwards they probably conducted a series of mild and controlled tests on the guy to see what the effects on the city were, and only then did they conclude that there was a definite correlation between the two.
It's probably Euclid because there's no telling how the people of the town would react to certain situations. If the guy lead a terrorist attack, would the Foundation have a townful of possibly anomalously augmented people plotting acts of terrorism? If he died, would the entire town fall down dead? It's unpredictable, hence it's Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, a guy smuggles a rodent into Argentina (a rodent that is already indigenous to Argentina, no less) and this captures the interest of the Foundation? And then they cross-referenced this with an interpretive dance in some farm town several thousands of miles away? Apart from the fact that they both involve porcupines, these two events have nothing to do with each other and are so utterly trivial and uninteresting on their own that I can't possibly fathom how you get a Euclid class from this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the general idea, but quite honestly, tl;dr. I wish the "tale-ier" parts had been extracted into their own, separate thing, to leave the body of the article standing on its own. After the second one, and being somewhat at a loss for how the events relate to the art projects in Dotson, I felt like I had the gist and couldn't see a reason for the rest of the article being there. Also, I'm still not a fan of the inter-Foundation publication footnotes. The writing's above reproach, but the structure needs some rethinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fucking beautiful and I teared up after reading it. This is not something that happens at all often. Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, +1. You've given us snapshots of this incredibly interesting, quasi-messianic figure who endeavors to make the world around him more interesting. Pablo has minor delusions of grandeur, but seems like the type of person who is aware of those delusions and is therefore immune to the more unpleasant effects of them, like arrogance.

He's an artist in a city where that probably doesn't count for much. I know that when I wrote more often, I'd have an idea in my head that someday I had to have fans, admirers, at least a big audience, but most people will probably never give a crap about my scrawlings. I know that I write for pride, and for approval, and for my implicit knowledge of that approval. Not the most noble of motivations, but it probably drives a lot of us.

What I love about Pablo's situation is that he seems to get joy out of simply inspiring art, and that limited to his surrounding community. But apparently, through no effort of his own, he did have a far-reaching effect on the world, and he died without ever knowing anything about it. And he felt no poorer for it.

tl;dr Your made-up person is very inspiring, and he feels very real, and I'm actually a little jealous of him.

Secondly, I wish there had been more clear connections between Pablo's actions and the "output" in Dotson. I felt like I had to stretch to understand most of the connections, and that a Foundation research team would have to be really looking hard to justify calling many of them connections - the portraits being an obvious exception, of course. Maybe it's just me, and it didn't detract from the story, per se. I think I could have used more cut-and-dry examples of "Yep, that's anomalous," and more early-on, to justify the Foundation's continued interest after the porcupine incident.

Finally, thank you oh so very much for not making Pablo's last words be "Are We Cool Yet?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is incredibly long, to the point where it would irritate me or even ruin the piece in a normal article - but the quality of this and the story it tells makes the length well worth it. It feels like I'm reading a novel more than an SCP article, but that is by no means a bad thing in this case. Safe to say this is already one of my favorite articles, +1 and thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I'm willing to forgive the different tone here - My headcanon is that clinical tone isn't strictly enforced in-universe, and is really just a product of the Foundation's atmosphere. Agent Esquivel is embedded deep within a completely different culture, so it makes sense it would affect his writing.

As for how it was discovered…

Mr. Izquierdo came to the attention of Foundation assets based in the Buenos Aires Regional Office in 1965, when he was arrested by Uruguay's Interior Police for attempting to smuggle live specimens of the Paraguaian hairy dwarf porcupine into Argentina. This coincided with a file report of an individual in the United States region of Nebraska conducting what was termed a "performance art" demonstration of an extended tango dance routine while his partner pierced him thirty-three times with porcupine quills.

I can't imagine these sorts of porcupine-related incidents being that common, so two happening in the same day would probably raise a few eyebrows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fucking fantastic work, Kalinin. Splendid characterization and portrayal of Foundation workers who are not soulless husks. SCP-348 didn't faze me; nor did those ships that the GOC sunk. This skip, though…
+1, man.
